 {"id":"bd-jfkk","title":"Audit and enhance ingest loop to provide all status metadata without Lua","description":"## Overview

Verify that wa's existing ingest/polling loop already provides all the metadata that STATUS_UPDATE_LUA was sending, and enhance it if needed. The goal is to confirm we lose nothing by removing the Lua hook.

## Current STATUS_UPDATE_LUA Payload

The Lua snippet sends this JSON to \`wa event --from-status\`:
\`\`\`json
{
  "schema_version": 0,
  "pane_id": 123,
  "domain": "local",
  "title": "zsh",
  "cursor": {"row": 10, "col": 5},
  "dimensions": {"rows": 24, "cols": 80},
  "is_alt_screen": false,
  "ts": 1706123456789
}
\`\`\`

## What Ingest Loop Already Provides

wa's ingest loop (crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs) polls \`wezterm cli list --format json\`:

\`\`\`json
[
  {
    "window_id": 0,
    "tab_id": 0,
    "pane_id": 0,
    "workspace": "default",
    "size": "80x24",        // ✅ dimensions available
    "title": "zsh",         // ✅ title available  
    "cwd": "/home/user",    // ✅ cwd available (bonus!)
    "domain": "local",      // ✅ domain available (new in WezTerm)
    "is_active": true       // ✅ active state available
  }
]
\`\`\`

## Gap Analysis

| Field | Lua Provides | CLI Provides | Action Needed |
|-------|-------------|--------------|---------------|
| pane_id | ✅ | ✅ | None |
| domain | ✅ | ✅ | None |
| title | ✅ | ✅ | None |
| dimensions | ✅ (rows/cols) | ✅ (80x24 string) | Parse "80x24" → (80, 24) |
| cursor position | ✅ | ❌ | See notes below |
| is_alt_screen | ✅ | ❌ | Handled by bd-2v4t (escape sequence detection) |
| cwd | ❌ | ✅ | Bonus - we get MORE data |

### Cursor Position

The cursor position was rarely used by wa. Options:
1. **Remove dependency**: Confirm no wa code actually needs cursor position
2. **On-demand query**: If ever needed, \`wezterm cli get-text --pane-id X\` includes cursor info
3. **Escape sequence parsing**: Could track cursor from CSI sequences (complex, probably overkill)

**Recommendation**: Audit wa codebase to confirm cursor position is unused, document as unavailable in polling mode.

## Implementation Tasks

### 1. Parse Dimensions String
\`\`\`rust
fn parse_dimensions(size: &str) -> Option<(u16, u16)> {
    let parts: Vec<&str> = size.split('x').collect();
    if parts.len() == 2 {
        let cols = parts[0].parse().ok()?;
        let rows = parts[1].parse().ok()?;
        Some((cols, rows))
    } else {
        None
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 2. Verify Domain Field Available
Check WezTerm version requirements — \`domain\` field was added in WezTerm 20230712+.
Add fallback for older versions (default to "local").

### 3. Audit Cursor Usage
Search codebase for any dependency on cursor position:
\`\`\`bash
rg "cursor" crates/wa-core/src/
\`\`\`

### 4. Update PaneInfo Struct
Ensure ingest.rs PaneInfo struct includes all fields:
\`\`\`rust
pub struct PaneInfo {
    pub pane_id: u64,
    pub title: String,
    pub domain: String,
    pub cwd: Option<String>,
    pub dimensions: (u16, u16),  // (cols, rows)
    pub is_active: bool,
    pub is_alt_screen: bool,  // From ScreenStateTracker
}
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All STATUS_UPDATE_LUA fields available without Lua (except cursor if unused)
- [ ] Dimensions parsed from "WxH" string format
- [ ] Domain field handled with fallback for old WezTerm
- [ ] Cursor position usage audited and documented
- [ ] PaneInfo struct updated
- [ ] Unit tests for dimension parsing
- [ ] Integration test: ingest loop provides complete metadata

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-2v4t (alt-screen detection) for is_alt_screen field

## Files to Modify

- crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs — PaneInfo struct and parsing
- crates/wa-core/src/wezterm.rs — CLI output parsing

## References

- wezterm cli list: https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/cli/cli/list.html

### Findings:

**2. Dimensions** ✅

- `effective_domain()`, `effective_title()`, `parsed_cwd()` helper methods exist

### Action Needed:
None for data availability. The next task (bd-30gj) can safely remove STATUS_UPDATE_LUA since all metadata is already available via CLI + escape sequence detection.

- `alt_screen`: From PaneEntry.is_alt_screen (Lua IPC source - will become stale)
- `cursor_alt_screen`: From PaneCursor.in_alt_screen (escape sequence detection)

 {"id":"bd-mwst","title":"Document Lua removal: CHANGELOG, migration guide, architecture update","description":"## Overview

Update all documentation to reflect the removal of STATUS_UPDATE_LUA and the new polling-based architecture. This ensures users understand the change and can migrate smoothly.

## Documentation Updates Required

### 1. CHANGELOG.md

Add entry for the version that removes STATUS_UPDATE_LUA:

\`\`\`markdown
## [0.2.0] - 2026-XX-XX

### Changed

- **BREAKING**: Removed Lua-based status update hook (\`update-status\`)
  - Dramatically improves WezTerm performance by eliminating high-frequency Lua callbacks
  - Pane metadata now obtained via polling (\`wezterm cli list\`)
  - Alt-screen detection now via escape sequence parsing (more reliable)

### Removed

- \`wa event --from-status\` CLI command (internal, not public API)
- STATUS_UPDATE_LUA snippet from \`wa setup\` output

### Migration

If you previously ran \`wa setup\`, run it again to update your wezterm.lua:

\`\`\`bash
wa setup --wezterm
\`\`\`

This will automatically remove the deprecated Lua code from the WA-managed block.
\`\`\`

### 2. README.md / AGENTS.md

Update any references to status updates:

- Remove mentions of "real-time status updates"
- Update architecture diagram if it shows Lua→wa flow
- Add note about polling-based pane metadata

### 3. PLAN.md / PLAN_CODEX.md

Update the specification:

**Section to update:**
- §2.7 (Status update specification) — mark as deprecated/removed
- Appendix E.1 — remove STATUS_UPDATE_LUA documentation

**Add new section:**
- §X.X Polling-based pane metadata
- Explain how alt-screen detection works via escape sequences

### 4. Architecture Documentation

Create or update architecture docs explaining:

1. **Before (Lua-based)**:
   \`\`\`
   WezTerm update-status event (60Hz)
     → Lua handler (runs every event)
       → Rate limit check
         → JSON serialization
           → wezterm.background_child_process
             → wa event --from-status
               → IPC to watcher
   \`\`\`

2. **After (Polling-based)**:
   \`\`\`
   wa watcher ingest loop (configurable interval)
     → wezterm cli list --format json
       → Parse pane metadata
     → ScreenStateTracker.process_output()
       → Parse alt-screen escape sequences
   \`\`\`

### 5. Inline Code Documentation

Update doc comments in affected files:

- crates/wa-core/src/setup.rs: Update module-level docs
- crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs: Document polling-based metadata
- crates/wa-core/src/screen_state.rs: Document escape sequence detection

### 6. Migration Guide

Create a dedicated migration section (can be in README or separate file):

\`\`\`markdown
## Migrating from wa 0.1.x to 0.2.x

### What Changed

In 0.2.0, we removed the Lua-based status update hook to dramatically improve WezTerm performance.

### Impact

- **No action required** for most users
- If you have custom integrations with \`wa event --from-status\`, see below

### Steps to Upgrade

1. **Update wa** to the latest version
2. **Re-run setup** to update your wezterm.lua:
   \`\`\`bash
   wa setup --wezterm
   \`\`\`
3. **Restart WezTerm** to pick up config changes

### Verifying the Migration

After upgrading, your wezterm.lua's WA-managed block should NOT contain:
- \`wezterm.on('update-status'\`
- \`wa_last_status_update\`
- \`WA_STATUS_UPDATE_INTERVAL_MS\`

It SHOULD still contain:
- \`wezterm.on('user-var-changed'\` (for agent signaling)

### Breaking Changes

- \`wa event --from-status\` command removed
- StatusUpdate IPC message type removed
- These were internal implementation details, not public APIs
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] CHANGELOG.md updated with breaking change notice
- [ ] README.md updated (if applicable)
- [ ] AGENTS.md updated (if applicable)
- [ ] PLAN.md §2.7 marked as deprecated
- [ ] Architecture documentation updated
- [ ] Migration guide written
- [ ] Code doc comments updated
- [ ] All docs spell-checked and links verified

- Depends on: bd-o2h4 (E2E tests passing, feature complete)

- README.md (if applicable)
- AGENTS.md (if applicable)

- PLAN_CODEX.md
- docs/migration-0.2.md (new, optional)
- crates/wa-core/src/setup.rs (doc comments)
- crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs (doc comments)

 {"id":"bd-o2h4","title":"Update E2E tests: remove status_update scenario, add alt-screen detection test","description":"## Overview

Update the E2E test suite to reflect the removal of STATUS_UPDATE_LUA and addition of alt-screen detection via escape sequences.

## Current E2E Test State

The E2E test harness (scripts/e2e_test.sh) has a "status_update" scenario that:
1. Writes a minimal wezterm.lua with STATUS_UPDATE_LUA
2. Spawns a pane
3. Sends a status update via \`wa event --from-status\`
4. Verifies the watcher receives it

This scenario will become invalid and must be removed.

## Changes Required

### 1. Remove status_update Scenario

In SCENARIO_REGISTRY (~line 340):
\`\`\`bash
# REMOVE this entry:
"status_update:Validate status update forwarding lane (wezterm.lua -> wa event -> watcher)"
\`\`\`

Remove the entire \`run_scenario_status_update()\` function (~lines 2550-2650).

### 2. Add alt-screen Detection Scenario

New scenario: "alt_screen_detection"

Purpose: Verify wa detects alt-screen transitions via escape sequence parsing (not Lua).

Test flow:
\`\`\`bash
run_scenario_alt_screen_detection() {
    # 1. Start wa watch
    # 2. Spawn a pane
    # 3. Send ESC[?1049h (enter alt-screen) to pane
    # 4. Verify wa detects alt-screen = true
    # 5. Send ESC[?1049l (leave alt-screen)
    # 6. Verify wa detects alt-screen = false
}
\`\`\`

Implementation sketch:
\`\`\`bash
run_scenario_alt_screen_detection() {
    local scenario_dir="$1"
    local wait_timeout="${TEST_WAIT_TIMEOUT:-30}"
    
    log_step "Starting wa watch..."
    wa watch --foreground > "$scenario_dir/wa_watch.log" 2>&1 &
    local wa_pid=$!
    sleep 2
    
    log_step "Spawning test pane..."
    local pane_id=$(wezterm cli spawn --cwd /tmp -- bash)
    sleep 1
    
    log_step "Verifying initial state (not alt-screen)..."
    local state=$(wa robot state --format json)
    local is_alt=$(echo "$state" | jq -r ".data.panes[] | select(.pane_id == $pane_id) | .is_alt_screen")
    if [[ "$is_alt" != "false" ]]; then
        log_fail "Expected is_alt_screen=false initially, got: $is_alt"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_step "Sending alt-screen enter sequence..."
    # ESC [ ? 1049 h
    printf '\e[?1049h' | wezterm cli send-text --pane-id "$pane_id" --no-paste
    sleep 0.5  # Allow detection
    
    log_step "Verifying alt-screen detected..."
    state=$(wa robot state --format json)
    is_alt=$(echo "$state" | jq -r ".data.panes[] | select(.pane_id == $pane_id) | .is_alt_screen")
    if [[ "$is_alt" != "true" ]]; then
        log_fail "Expected is_alt_screen=true after ESC[?1049h, got: $is_alt"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_step "Sending alt-screen leave sequence..."
    # ESC [ ? 1049 l
    printf '\e[?1049l' | wezterm cli send-text --pane-id "$pane_id" --no-paste
    sleep 0.5
    
    log_step "Verifying alt-screen exited..."
    state=$(wa robot state --format json)
    is_alt=$(echo "$state" | jq -r ".data.panes[] | select(.pane_id == $pane_id) | .is_alt_screen")
    if [[ "$is_alt" != "false" ]]; then
        log_fail "Expected is_alt_screen=false after ESC[?1049l, got: $is_alt"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_pass "Alt-screen detection via escape sequences works!"
    cleanup_pane "$pane_id"
    kill "$wa_pid" 2>/dev/null
}
\`\`\`

### 3. Update uservar_forwarding Scenario

Ensure the uservar_forwarding scenario still works (it should, we're only removing status updates).

Verify it doesn't depend on status update functionality.

### 4. Add Regression Test: No Lua Status Hook

New scenario: "no_lua_status_hook"

Purpose: Verify wezterm.lua doesn't contain STATUS_UPDATE_LUA after setup.

run_scenario_no_lua_status_hook() {

## Purpose
Comprehensive testing for the harmonized workflow subcommand structure:
- `wa robot workflow run <name> <pane>` (syntax change from bd-2kl8)
- `wa robot workflow list` (implementation in wa-35l)
- `wa robot workflow status <id>` (implementation in wa-7dd)
- `wa robot workflow abort <id>` (implementation in wa-55y)
- `wa robot events --unhandled` (alias from bd-2rwq)

## Unit Tests

### CLI Parsing Tests (Already Implemented)
```rust
#[test]
fn test_workflow_run_subcommand_parsing() {
    // Old syntax should fail
    let result = parse_args(&["wa", "robot", "workflow", "handle_compaction", "3"]);
    assert!(result.is_err(), "Implicit run should be rejected");
    
    // New syntax should work
    let result = parse_args(&["wa", "robot", "workflow", "run", "handle_compaction", "3"]);
    assert!(result.is_ok());
}

#[test]
fn test_unhandled_flag_alias() {
    // Both flags should parse to same value
    let result1 = parse_args(&["wa", "robot", "events", "--unhandled"]);
    let result2 = parse_args(&["wa", "robot", "events", "--unhandled-only"]);
    assert_eq!(result1.unhandled_only, result2.unhandled_only);
}
```

### JSON Schema Validation Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_workflow_list_json_schema() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list"]);
    validate_against_schema(&output, "wa-robot-workflow-list.json");
    assert!(output["data"]["workflows"].is_array());
}

#[test]
fn test_workflow_status_json_schema() {
    let exec_id = start_test_workflow();
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "status", &exec_id]);
    validate_against_schema(&output, "wa-robot-workflow-status.json");
}

#[test]
fn test_workflow_abort_json_schema() {
    let exec_id = start_test_workflow();
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "abort", &exec_id]);
    validate_against_schema(&output, "wa-robot-workflow-abort.json");
}
```

### Error Code Stability Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_workflow_not_found_error() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "status", "nonexistent"]);
    assert_eq!(output["error"]["code"], "E_EXECUTION_NOT_FOUND");
}
```

## E2E Tests

### Scenario: Full Workflow Lifecycle
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# e2e_workflow_lifecycle.sh
set -euo pipefail
LOG="$ARTIFACT_DIR/workflow_lifecycle.log"
log() { echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] $*" | tee -a "$LOG"; }

log "=== Workflow Lifecycle E2E ==="

# 1. List workflows
log "Listing workflows..."
LIST=$(wa robot workflow list)
log "List: $LIST"
echo "$LIST" | jq -e '.ok == true' || { log "FAIL: list"; exit 1; }

# 2. Run a workflow (dry-run)
log "Starting workflow (dry-run)..."

EXEC_ID=$(echo "$RUN" | jq -r '.data.execution_id // empty')
if [ -n "$EXEC_ID" ]; then
    log "Checking status for $EXEC_ID..."
    STATUS=$(wa robot workflow status "$EXEC_ID")
    log "Status: $STATUS"
fi

log "=== PASS: workflow_lifecycle ==="
```

### Scenario: Events Unhandled Flag

Implement the wa-side Unix socket listener that receives events from vendored WezTerm's native integration. This replaces the Lua→CLI→IPC path with direct socket communication.

## Implementation

### 1. Event Listener Module

Create \`crates/wa-core/src/native_events.rs\`:

\`\`\`rust
use std::path::PathBuf;
use tokio::net::{UnixListener, UnixStream};
use tokio::sync::broadcast;

/// Events received from native WezTerm integration.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub enum NativeEvent {
    PaneOutput {
        pane_id: u64,
        data: Vec<u8>,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    StateChange {
        pane_id: u64,
        title: String,
        rows: u16,
        cols: u16,
        is_alt_screen: bool,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    UserVarChanged {
        pane_id: u64,
        name: String,
        value: String,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    PaneCreated {
        pane_id: u64,
        domain: String,
        cwd: Option<String>,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    PaneDestroyed {
        pane_id: u64,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
}

/// Listener for native WezTerm events.
pub struct NativeEventListener {
    socket_path: PathBuf,
    listener: UnixListener,
    event_tx: broadcast::Sender<NativeEvent>,
}

impl NativeEventListener {
    /// Create and bind the event listener socket.
    pub async fn bind(socket_path: PathBuf) -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Remove stale socket if exists
        let _ = std::fs::remove_file(&socket_path);
        
        // Create parent directory
        if let Some(parent) = socket_path.parent() {
            std::fs::create_dir_all(parent)?;
        }
        
        let listener = UnixListener::bind(&socket_path)?;
        let (event_tx, _) = broadcast::channel(1024);
        
        Ok(Self {
            socket_path,
            listener,
            event_tx,
        })
    }
    
    /// Subscribe to events.
    pub fn subscribe(&self) -> broadcast::Receiver<NativeEvent> {
        self.event_tx.subscribe()
    }
    
    /// Run the event listener loop.
    pub async fn run(&self) -> Result<(), Error> {
        loop {
            let (stream, _) = self.listener.accept().await?;
            let event_tx = self.event_tx.clone();
            
            tokio::spawn(async move {
                if let Err(e) = handle_connection(stream, event_tx).await {
                    tracing::warn!("Native event connection error: {}", e);
                }
            });
        }
    }
}

async fn handle_connection(
    stream: UnixStream,
    event_tx: broadcast::Sender<NativeEvent>,
) -> Result<(), Error> {
    use tokio::io::{AsyncBufReadExt, BufReader};
    
    let reader = BufReader::new(stream);
    let mut lines = reader.lines();
    
    while let Some(line) = lines.next_line().await? {
        match parse_event(&line) {
            Ok(event) => {
                let _ = event_tx.send(event);  // Ignore if no subscribers
            }
            Err(e) => {
                tracing::debug!("Failed to parse native event: {}", e);
            }
        }
    }
    
    Ok(())
}

fn parse_event(json: &str) -> Result<NativeEvent, Error> {
    let value: serde_json::Value = serde_json::from_str(json)?;
    
    match value.get("type").and_then(|t| t.as_str()) {
        Some("pane_output") => Ok(NativeEvent::PaneOutput {
            pane_id: value["pane_id"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
            data: base64::decode(value["data"].as_str().unwrap_or(""))?,
            timestamp: value["ts"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
        }),
        Some("state_change") => Ok(NativeEvent::StateChange {
            pane_id: value["pane_id"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
            title: value["state"]["title"].as_str().unwrap_or("").to_string(),
            rows: value["state"]["rows"].as_u64().unwrap_or(24) as u16,
            cols: value["state"]["cols"].as_u64().unwrap_or(80) as u16,
            is_alt_screen: value["state"]["is_alt_screen"].as_bool().unwrap_or(false),
            timestamp: value["ts"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
        }),
        // ... other event types
        _ => Err(Error::UnknownEventType),
    }
}

### 2. Integration with Watcher

Modify \`crates/wa-core/src/watcher.rs\` or equivalent:

\`\`\`rust
impl Watcher {
    pub async fn run_with_native_events(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        let native_listener = NativeEventListener::bind(
            self.config.native_event_socket.clone()
        ).await?;
        
        let mut native_rx = native_listener.subscribe();
        
        // Spawn listener task
        let listener_handle = tokio::spawn(async move {
            native_listener.run().await
        });
        
        loop {
            tokio::select! {
                // Existing poll loop
                _ = self.poll_interval.tick() => {
                    self.poll_panes().await?;
                }
                
                // Native events
                Ok(event) = native_rx.recv() => {
                    self.handle_native_event(event).await?;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    
    async fn handle_native_event(&mut self, event: NativeEvent) -> Result<()> {
        match event {
            NativeEvent::PaneOutput { pane_id, data, .. } => {
                // Feed to pattern detection
                self.pattern_engine.process(&data);
                // Feed to screen state tracker
                self.screen_tracker.process_output(pane_id, &data);
            }
            NativeEvent::StateChange { pane_id, is_alt_screen, .. } => {
                // Update pane state directly (no polling needed)
                self.pane_states.update_alt_screen(pane_id, is_alt_screen);
            }
            NativeEvent::UserVarChanged { pane_id, name, value, .. } => {
                // Same handling as IPC user-var events
                self.handle_user_var(pane_id, &name, &value).await?;
            }
            // ... other events
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}

## Goal
Make sure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` is a comprehensive, maintained suite that:
- runs every **registered** E2E scenario (not just the ones under `wa-4vx.10.*`)
- produces stable artifacts per case
- prints a clear PASS/FAIL summary

This task exists to avoid coupling “runner quality” to “suite completeness”.

## Requirements
- Maintain a single authoritative registry of cases (names + descriptions).
- The registry must also record (at least):
  - prerequisites (e.g., requires WezTerm, requires docker, requires feature flags)
  - whether the case is part of the default `--all` run
  - a human-readable “why this case exists” note (what it protects)

- `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` iterates that registry.
- Each case has:
  - deterministic steps
  - explicit assertions
  - artifacts directory
  - clear failure summary

## Cross-repo case coverage (important)
Some E2E scenarios live outside `wa-4vx.10.*` (for example setup safety, new IPC lanes, and UX overlays):
- `wa-nu4.3.3.10` (wa setup idempotency)
- `wa-4vx.2.7.3` (status_update IPC lane)
- `wa-nu4.1.6.4` (pane reservations)
- `wa-0go.7` (natural-language event descriptions)
- `wa-5em.9` (rollback visualization tests/E2E)
- `wa-dug.7` (environment detection + recommendations)
- `wa-tp4.8` (suggestion engine)
- `wa-nu4.4.3.5` (distributed E2E tests; non-default unless distributed feature enabled)
- `wa-9lh` (quick-fix suggestions E2E)
- `wa-am5` (dry-run mode E2E)
- `wa-jl5` (notification webhook delivery E2E)
- `wa-ugg` (timeline correlation E2E)
- `wa-p3i` (watch-and-notify mode E2E)

Suite completeness means the registry accounts for these cases too.
Implementation options (either is acceptable; pick the simplest):
- unify everything under `./scripts/e2e_test.sh` as runnable `--case` entries, OR
- allow registry entries to delegate to another script (adapter entry) while still producing the same artifacts contract.

## Change discipline
- Adding a new E2E case requires updating the registry.
- CI should catch:
  - “case exists but not in registry”
  - “case is in registry but not included in default suite” (unless explicitly tagged as non-default with justification)

## Registry
- This bead *is* the registry. Update the case list here when new E2E cases are added.

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A developer can run `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` and trust it covers the full default suite.
- The suite remains usable even as cases evolve across phases.
- `wa-nu4.3.3.10`, `wa-4vx.2.7.3`, `wa-nu4.1.6.4`, `wa-0go.7`, `wa-5em.9`, `wa-dug.7`, and `wa-tp4.8` are represented in the case registry (directly or via adapter), with stable artifacts.


## Testing
- Registry correctness tests:
  - Add a check that enumerates available E2E cases and fails if any are missing from the registry.
  - Add a check that fails if the registry references a non-existent case.

- `--all` behavior tests:
  - Include a small dummy registry in test mode to verify:
    - ordering and selection flags
    - summary format
    - per-case artifact dir naming is stable

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.21","title":"E2E script: wa stop shuts down watcher (lock released, restart works)","description":"# Task: E2E script — wa stop

## Goal
Validate the user-facing shutdown path:
- wa watch is running
- wa stop stops it gracefully
- the lock is released
- restart works

This is a key UX reliability story for real users.

## Scenario
- Start mux server and spawn a dummy pane that prints a unique token.
- Start wa watch in workspace A.
- Wait until the token is persisted (wa robot search).
- Run wa stop --workspace A.

Assertions
- wa stop returns success.
- wa watch process exits.
- workspace lock is released.
- Starting wa watch again in workspace A succeeds.

## Artifacts
- wa watch logs
- wa stop logs/output
- effective config

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic.
- Failures clearly indicate whether the bug is in:
  - lock metadata
  - signal handling
  - shutdown flushing


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add a negative case: `wa stop` when no watcher is running must return a clear, non-destructive message (and still exit 0/1 consistently as defined).
  - Assert that lock release is proven by attempting a second start (not by checking files alone).

- Artifact validation:
  - Include watcher PID/lock metadata evidence in artifacts so failures are actionable.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.22","title":"E2E script: wa doctor (healthy/broken) with verbose logs + artifacts","description":"# Task: E2E script — wa doctor

## Goal
Prove `wa doctor` works end-to-end as a **deterministic** health-check surface with **excellent logs/artifacts**.

Unit tests can validate individual checks, but this E2E case ensures the full command (arg parsing → config resolution → checks → output) behaves correctly and remains user-friendly.

## Scenario(s)

### Scenario A: Healthy workspace
- Create a fresh temp workspace (isolated DB/log dirs).
- Ensure stubbed dependencies report “healthy”:
  - wezterm CLI available + returns a plausible `wezterm cli list` output
  - DB opens and schema version matches
- Run:
  - `wa doctor --format json`

### Scenario B: Broken workspace (actionable failures)
Run doctor against controlled failures, one at a time:
- Missing wezterm CLI.
- Workspace not writable.
- DB cannot be opened / schema mismatch.

## Assertions
- Exit codes are correct and stable:
  - healthy → `0`
  - broken → non-zero
- JSON output is machine-parseable and contains:
  - a concise overall status
  - per-check status + actionable remediation hints
- No secrets appear in:
  - stdout JSON
  - stderr logs
  - artifact files

## Artifacts / logging contract
- Capture and store per-scenario artifacts in the standard E2E harness layout:
  - stdout (raw)
  - stderr (raw)
  - resolved workspace paths
  - any generated diagnostic files
  - timing summary (start/end + durations per step)

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Script is deterministic and can run in CI.
- When it fails, the artifact bundle is sufficient to debug quickly.

## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Ensure each “broken workspace” scenario fails for the intended reason (not cascading unrelated failures).
  - Add assertions that the remediation hints include stable `code` values so automation can key off them.

  - Ensure the script stores both the raw doctor output and a normalized “check summary” extraction for quick debugging.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T12:18:28.342228068Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T01:59:43.823781370Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T01:59:43.823640749Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-diagnostics","area-e2e","area-tests","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10.11","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.6.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.3.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"}]}
-{"id":"wa-4vx.10.23","title":"E2E script: accounts refresh (fake caut) + pick preview + redaction","description":"# Task: E2E test script — accounts refresh + pick preview (fake caut)

## Goal
Validate the accounts/usage integration end-to-end without network access:
- `wa robot accounts refresh` invokes `caut` via wrapper, updates DB mirror
- `wa robot accounts` shows deterministic account state + recommended pick
- logs/artifacts remain secret-safe and actionable

This protects a high-risk integration surface (external CLI drift + error handling).

## Scenario
Run in an isolated temp workspace with a stubbed `caut` executable on `PATH`.

### Setup
- Create a temp workspace dir (isolated DB/log paths).
- Create a temp `bin/` dir with a **fake caut** script that:
  - supports `caut refresh --service openai --format json`
  - supports `caut usage --service openai --format json`
  - returns fixture JSON for a few accounts
  - can be toggled to return:
    - non-zero exit + stderr message
    - invalid JSON

### Steps
1. Run: `wa robot accounts refresh --service openai`
   - Assert: exit 0, output schema is valid, DB mirror updated.
2. Run: `wa robot accounts --service openai`
   - Assert: deterministic ordering, `recommended` matches policy.
3. Negative path:
   - Make fake caut fail (exit != 0) and re-run refresh
   - Assert: actionable error + remediation hint; no secrets leaked.

## Assertions
- Output stability:
  - Both commands validate against robot schemas.
  - Ordering is deterministic.
- Correctness:
  - DB mirror reflects fixture values.
  - recommended selection matches selection policy (threshold + LRU).
- Safety:
  - no secrets in stdout JSON, stderr logs, or artifact files.

## Test runner integration
- Must run via the E2E runner (`./scripts/e2e_test.sh`) and follow the harness artifacts contract.

## Artifacts/logging
- Capture:
  - stdout/stderr for each command
  - effective config snapshot (workspace paths)
  - DB snapshot or query output proving accounts table contents
  - runner timing + step markers

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic and CI-friendly (no network, no real services).
- Failures produce artifacts sufficient to debug wrapper parsing vs DB update vs selection logic.


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Assert the fake `caut` was actually invoked (e.g., via a marker file or captured stderr) to avoid accidentally calling a real binary.
  - Ensure the negative-path variant (invalid JSON / non-zero exit) fails for the correct reason and surfaces a stable error code.

- Artifact validation:
  - Include the fake caut stdout/stderr in artifacts so parsing drift is diagnosable.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.24","title":"E2E script: rules list/test + pack linter (fixture-first drift)","description":"# Task: E2E script — rules list/test + pack linter

## Goal
Validate the “rules toolchain” end-to-end (CLI wiring + stable JSON + fixtures):
- `wa robot rules list` returns stable rule IDs + metadata
- `wa robot rules test "<text>"` returns a stable match trace
- pack linter/drift workflow is runnable and produces actionable output

This protects against silent drift in rule packs and prevents brittle agent integrations.

## Scenario
Run entirely offline using fixture packs/corpus.

### Steps
1. Run: `wa robot rules list`
   - Assert: output schema valid; includes at least the core packs; IDs are stable.
2. Run: `wa robot rules test "<known fixture line>"`
   - Assert: match trace contains expected `rule_id` and extracted fields.
3. Run pack linter (command name flexible):
   - Assert: every rule has at least one fixture; stable IDs; no obviously-dangerous regex.

## Assertions
- Output stability:
  - command outputs validate against robot schemas
  - deterministic ordering
- Correctness:
  - known fixture triggers exactly the expected rule
  - negative fixture does not match
- UX:
  - failures include actionable remediation hints (where to add fixtures, which rule drifted)

## Artifacts/logging
- Capture:
  - stdout/stderr for each command
  - any linter report files
  - runner timing + step markers

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Script is deterministic and CI-friendly.
- When it fails, artifacts make it obvious whether the break is:
  - schema/output drift
  - pack data regression
  - linter enforcement


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Include a deliberately broken fixture pack variant to prove the linter actually fails (missing fixture, duplicate rule id, invalid regex).
  - Assert the `rules test` trace includes stable field names so agents can parse it.

- Artifact validation:
  - Ensure the linter report is always written (even on failure) and included in artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.25","title":"E2E script: command safety gate blocks destructive-looking sends (optional dcg)","description":"# Task: E2E test script — command safety gate

## Goal
Prove end-to-end that `wa` refuses (deny or require-approval) to inject destructive-looking commands, even when the target pane is prompt-active.

This validates PLAN.md §14.2 “Command Safety Gate”.

## Scenario
- Start mux server and watcher in a dedicated workspace.
- Spawn a dummy pane that is “safe to type into” (prompt-active markers) and echoes input.
- Run a set of sends that include:
  - safe prompt-like text (should be allowed)
  - safe command-like text (allowed)
  - destructive command-like text (deny or require-approval, depending on config)

Optional variant:
- With a stubbed `dcg` binary on PATH:
  - simulate dcg allow
  - simulate dcg deny

## Assertions
- A known destructive string (e.g., `rm -rf /`) is **not** injected:
  - robot/human output indicates Deny/RequireApproval with stable error codes
  - audit records the attempted action and the rule id/reason
  - pane output does not contain the destructive string

- A safe string *is* injected:
  - pane echoes it
  - audit records allow

- dcg integration behavior (if enabled in test config):
  - dcg deny produces RequireApproval/Deny (as configured)
  - dcg allow does not block

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging/artifacts
- Must follow the E2E harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and use structured logs (`wa-4vx.6.5`).
- Artifacts must include:
  - stdout/stderr of commands
  - watcher logs
  - audit export slice
  - a grep-style proof that destructive strings do not appear in pane tails or artifact files

## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Include a control that proves the test would fail if the gate were disabled (e.g., flip config and assert injection occurs in that variant).
  - Explicitly scan *all* artifacts for the destructive string and fail if present.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The test is deterministic and CI-friendly.
- A regression in command safety gating fails loudly with artifacts that pinpoint:
  - classifier mistake
  - policy/rule evaluation mistake
  - dcg integration mistake

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.3","title":"Fuzz targets (cargo-fuzz): pack parser, OSC parser, FTS query safety","description":"# Task: Fuzz targets (cargo-fuzz)

## Goal
Harden parser/decoder surfaces via fuzz testing and prevent:
- panics
- infinite loops / pathological backtracking
- unbounded allocations

Fuzzing is especially valuable for wa because we ingest adversarial-ish terminal output and untrusted-ish config inputs.

## Targets (initial)
1) Pattern pack parsing
   - TOML/YAML parsing and validation
   - rule id uniqueness and required fields

2) OSC / marker parsing
   - OSC 133 prompt markers
   - any additional user-var lane formats

3) Search query handling
   - FTS query normalization and error paths
   - ensure malformed queries return structured errors (not panics)

## Harness requirements
- Use `cargo-fuzz` with libFuzzer.
- Provide small seed corpora for each target:
  - valid minimal examples
  - edge-case examples (empty, very long, unicode, invalid bytes)
- Ensure targets are deterministic and do not require filesystem/network.

## Crash handling conventions
- On crash, artifacts should be reproducible with a single command:
  - `cargo fuzz run <target> <artifact>`
- Keep logs minimal (fuzzing produces lots of iterations). If logging is needed, gate behind an env var.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Fuzzers run for a fixed time budget without crashes (local smoke is fine for v0; CI integration can follow).
- Known-bad malformed inputs (from bug reports) are added as seeds/regression cases.


## Testing
- Fuzz harness self-check:
  - Add a quick “smoke run” command for each target (short time budget) to ensure targets compile and execute.
  - Add at least one regression seed per target for previously-found issues (or synthetic known-bad inputs).

 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.3","title":"Persist workflow_executions + workflow_step_log; resume after restart from last completed step","description":"# Task: Persist workflow runs + resume logic

## Goal
Make workflows durable so `wa watch` can restart without losing where it was.

## Requirements
- On workflow start, create `workflow_executions` row.
- On each step:
  - mark step started with an input snapshot (redacted)
  - on completion mark completed with output snapshot (redacted)
  - on failure mark failed with error detail and attempt count
- On daemon restart:
  - find running/incomplete workflows
  - reconstruct state and resume from last completed step

## Idempotency notes
- Resuming must not repeat already-completed steps.
- Step results should be written before side effects whenever possible.

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - resume computes next step index correctly given a step log
- Integration tests:
  - simulate “kill mid-workflow” and verify:
    - already-completed steps are not re-run
    - workflow completes after restart

## Acceptance Criteria
- Kill `wa watch` mid-workflow and restart: it resumes without repeating already-completed steps.
- Step logs are sufficient to understand what happened without consulting raw terminal text.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.4","title":"WaitCondition implementation: Pattern wait + PaneIdle via OSC 133 (fallback heuristics if needed)","description":"# Task: WaitCondition implementation

## Goal
Implement `wait_for_condition` correctly, efficiently, and in a way that is shared across robot/workflow code paths.

This is one of the core reliability primitives:
- it’s how workflows avoid spamming
- it’s how workflows become resumable and debuggable
- it’s how “verified sends” become possible

## PaneIdle
Prefer deterministic shell integration:
- `PaneIdle` == observe OSC 133 prompt marker.

Fallback (only if shell integration missing):
- heuristic prompt match (explicitly marked as best-effort)

## Pattern wait
- Poll get-text (tail window) using the shared PaneWaiter (`wa-4vx.2.6`).
- Stop early on success.
- Return timeout error on deadline.

## Implementation notes
- Do not re-implement polling loops inside the workflow layer; reuse PaneWaiter so:
  - workflow waits, robot wait-for, and send verification behave identically
  - backoff and cancellation semantics are consistent

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Pattern wait succeeds/fails deterministically on synthetic fixtures.
  - Timeouts are honored precisely (no “wait forever” bugs).
  - Cancellation produces a stable outcome and a debuggable step log entry.
  - PaneIdle transitions are correct for OSC 133 sequences.
  - Fallback heuristics are only used when deterministic signals are missing (and are clearly reported).

- Integration tests:
  - A synthetic workflow run that includes at least one wait step records stable step logs and does not spam sends.

## Acceptance Criteria
- WaitFor works reliably on synthetic fixtures.
- Timeouts/cancellations produce stable, debuggable step logs.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.5","title":"Workflow runner: consume Detection events, select workflow, execute with policy gates","description":"# Task: Workflow runner/scheduler

## Goal
Bridge between detections/events and workflow executions.

## Responsibilities
- Subscribe to detection/event stream.
- Choose the highest-priority matching workflow.
- Enforce per-pane lock.
- Execute steps with:
  - policy checks before any input injection
  - bounded retries with backoff
  - step logging (durable)
  - clear “why did/didn’t we act?” outcomes

## Action execution architecture (important)
To avoid duplicated safety logic, the runner should not “roll its own” injection path. It should route any action attempt (send text, ctrl-c, etc) through a shared core helper that:
- calls PolicyEngine
- emits audit entries (when wired)
- performs the action only if allowed

This is how we ensure robot/human/workflow paths stay consistent.

## Event interaction
- The runner should cooperate with event semantics (`wa-nu4.1.1.10`):
  - set `in_progress` (if used)
  - mark handled on completion/abort/failure
  - avoid spamming repeat executions for the same event

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - workflow selection chooses correct workflow given a detection
  - lock prevents concurrent runs per pane
  - policy-denied action yields a stable denied outcome without calling send
- Integration tests:
  - synthetic event stream → creates workflow_executions + step logs

## Acceptance Criteria
- When a compaction detection arrives, `handle_compaction` workflow runs and records a completed execution.
- When policy denies an action, the workflow records an explainable denied/aborted result and does not inject input.

-{"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.6","title":"Data-driven workflow descriptors (YAML/TOML) for simple prompt injection sequences","description":"# Task: Data-driven workflow descriptors (YAML/TOML)

## Goal
Allow operator customization of **simple, safe workflows** without recompiling wa.

This is about *configuration-driven prompt sequences* ("wait for X, then send Y"), not complex logic.

## Scope (strict)
Only support a small, explicitly safe step vocabulary:
- `wait_for` (substring/regex) with timeout
- `sleep` (bounded)
- `send_text` (must go through PolicyEngine; can be denied)
- `send_ctrl` (limited set like Ctrl-C; also policy gated)

Non-goals:
- loops/conditionals
- arbitrary shell execution
- custom code hooks

## Descriptor model
- Versioned schema (e.g., `workflow_schema_version: 1`).
- Strong validation at load time:
  - unknown fields rejected
  - timeouts and sleep durations bounded
  - maximum number of steps bounded
  - step ids unique for audit/logging

## Execution integration
- Descriptors compile into the same internal step executor used by Rust workflows.
- Every send step uses the shared policy-gated injection helper.
- Every step writes a step log entry (same durability semantics as native workflows).

## Security & redaction
- Descriptor files may contain prompt text; treat them as potentially sensitive.
- Logs/audit should record only a redacted summary of sent text.

## Testing strategy
- Unit tests:
  - parsing + validation (good and bad fixtures)
  - schema version errors
  - bounding behavior (too many steps, too-long timeout) rejects

- Integration tests:
  - execute a sample descriptor against a fake pane/query environment
  - assert step logs emitted and policy gating is invoked

## Acceptance Criteria
- A sample YAML workflow can be loaded, validated, and executed.
- Invalid descriptors fail with precise error messages and remediation hints.
- Send steps are policy-gated and produce audit + step logs.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.8","title":"Integrate workflow action loop into `wa watch` (auto-handle) while preserving observe/act split","description":"# Task: Integrate workflows into `wa watch`

## Goal
Enable `wa watch --auto-handle` to run workflows in response to detections, while keeping the **observe** loop strictly passive and the **act** loop explicitly gated.

This is the moment `wa` stops being “just a recorder/search index” and becomes a safe automation runtime.

## Core invariants (non-negotiable)
- The observation loop remains passive:
  - no `send-text`, no control sequences, no workflow actions
  - if workflow code is not enabled, wa still captures + indexes reliably
- The action loop is explicit:
  - runs only when enabled by config/flags
  - always re-checks live pane state immediately before each action
  - every action attempt is policy-gated

## Deliverables
- `wa watch` starts a workflow runner/scheduler when configured.
- Config + flags:
  - enable/disable workflow runtime
  - allowlist which workflows may auto-run
  - max concurrent workflows (global + per-pane)
  - `--auto-handle` (opt-in) to act automatically
- Structured logs and audit correlation:
  - a workflow execution id is included on all logs for that run

## Failure and safety behavior
- If policy denies an action during a workflow:
  - workflow step is recorded as denied (not retried blindly)
  - the originating event remains visible to the user/robot as unhandled (or handled_aborted, depending on semantics in `wa-nu4.1.1.10`)
- If workflow engine errors:
  - observation loop continues running
  - action loop stops cleanly and reports an actionable error

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - workflow runner does not start when `--auto-handle` is off
  - action loop does not execute without explicit enable
- Integration tests:
  - given a synthetic detection stream, `wa watch` launches workflow execution and records execution rows
  - denied policy decisions never result in a send attempt
- E2E:
  - `wa-4vx.10.8` compaction workflow end-to-end
  - `wa-4vx.10.9` usage-limit workflow end-to-end
  - `wa-4vx.10.12` restart/resume idempotency

## Acceptance Criteria
- With `--auto-handle` enabled and compaction is detected, the workflow triggers exactly once per event (subject to dedupe/cooldown semantics).
- With `--auto-handle` disabled, detections/events are recorded but no sends occur.
- A denied policy decision prevents any input injection and produces a stable, explainable result (logs + audit/step log).

 {"id":"wa-nu4.1.1.9","title":"[Robot] `wa robot workflow` (run workflow by name, stable result schema)","description":"# Task: `wa robot workflow`

## Goal
Expose workflow execution to agents as a **stable robot command**.

Command:
- `wa robot workflow <name> <pane_id> [--force]`

## Why
- Agents need a single canonical entrypoint to request automation.
- MCP should delegate to the same underlying engine.

## Behavior
- Validate pane exists.
- Acquire per-pane lock.
- Run workflow via the runner.
- Emit a stable result envelope:
  - workflow name
  - pane_id
  - execution_id
  - status (completed/aborted/failed)
  - summary message
  - optional list of step results (short)

## Safety
- PolicyEngine gates any sends.
- `--force` is still policy-gated; it only bypasses workflow-level “already handled” checks.

## Testing
- Unit tests against synthetic workflows.
- JSON output validated against robot schema (`wa-4vx.7.10`).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Works against synthetic workflows in tests.
- If policy denies, the command returns a stable robot error and does not attempt to send.
